title: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis
display_title: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis
wikitext:
[]: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Apogee Perigee » Thu Nov 17, 2016 9:50 pm This hypothesis (JUST A HYPOTHESIS! I'm not declaring this as true!) is the idea of Kaworu being a father figure to Shinji and Rei being a mother figure. Freud's father complex is a complex which mainly has to do with the (obvious) aspects of a father. These aspects normally include a close friendship and mentoring for boys. Sound familiar? Kaworu acts as a mentor and close friend of Shinji's. He may also (in a more Jungian sense) be acting out his own desire to be Shinji's Senex. This reveals more about Kaworu and his obvious self-sacrificing nature. In a theological sense, it can be believed that Kaworu is a sort of Christlike figure. He sacrifices himself so that humanity can live. The Son or Christ is simply an earthly embodiment of the father. (Adam being the father). This further proves, in my belief, that Kaworu is Shinji's father figure. Rei, on the other hand, is much easier to prove. For one, she's a clone of his mother. Secondly, her calm voice and her "return to the womb" scene in third impact further portray a motherly figure, which could link to Sophia in gnosticism but that's for a different time. If you agree with me, tell me what you think! And if you don't, please tell me why! I'm always open to new ideas so if you think I'm wrong about something I'm open.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Sykes III » Fri Nov 18, 2016 1:30 pm Yeah, Rei is obvioulsy a mother figure, that much is made clear over and over in the series. The confusion between a love interest and a mother figure near the start of the series runs into Freudian territory, but resolves itself as Asuka is introduced as the true love interest and the relationship between Shinji and Rei shifts (except in Asuka's mind, who misinterprets it). As for Kaworu, well if Rei is the Mother, it makes sense to see Kaworu as fatherly, since he's presented as an opposing to parallel to Rei in almost all other aspects. Of course, it's always hard to make big sweeping statements about Kaworu since he's, y'know, not actually in much of the story. At least before Rebuild came along, anyway. Regardless of any symbolism with Kaworu, Gendo is indisputably the most important father figure in the story with absent dad Dr. Katsuragi coming in a distant second place (and of course, I could go into how Gendo and Katsuragi parallel each other in a way that enhances the parallel between Shinji and Misato and use that to talk about the parallel between Rei and Kaworu and use THAT to talk about Kaworu and Dr. Katsuragi, buuuuuttt....) the point I am getting at is that Kaworu's most important role is to contrast with Gendo (and EVERYONE ELSE in the cast) as someone who is actually nice, understanding, and open to Shinji, thus driving Shinji further into himself almost irrevocably after he is forced to kill him. That's his role, to be the Nice Person Who Has To Die. Which I guess backs up your Christ analog, which is a bit neat since other than the use of crosses themselves it's the only bit of New Testament in NGE, which otherwise confines most of it's flashy religious references to Genesis and Kabbalism.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Apogee Perigee » Fri Nov 18, 2016 2:09 pm Hi. Thanks for responding. You really helped me get it more.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Nov 20, 2016 1:31 am I think Kaworu is too convoluted to simply represent "the father". Similar to the way he bridges Adam and Lilith based life, I think Kaworu is an androgynous figure who bridges female and male. Despite the name, Adam is identified in NGE, by Kaworu no less, as a mother. This actually does makes sense -- Adam is the same sort of being as Lilith and the Evas, all of whom are essentially Mother Goddesses embodying the fluidic inner universe of life's origin to which so many long to return. (Including the Angels: "must those who are born from Adam return to Adam...?") If that sounds like gobbledygook, I'm referring to NGE's symbolic conflation of the human womb with origins on a much more cosmic scale: primordial soup in the world's ancient ocean, or even the "red space" of Paradise itself (the otherworldly Sea of LCL, seen in EoE). I would say that Kaworu's identity is more complicated than a straightforward Jesus analogy. The comparison is there, but NGE puts its own spin on it starting with Kaworu's origin. Rather than a masculine God impregnating a mortal woman, we have a mortal man (the donor, possibly Dr. Katsuragi) effectively impregnating a feminine God. The resultant Son ends up with the soul of his own Mother and the exact (palette-swapped) image of his Father. Kaworu is Mother, Father, and Son all at once. That's all I can write for now, but here's another post that explores these ideas a bit more if you're interested: post/848054/Welcome-Home/#848054

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by SoryuUberAlles » Sun Nov 20, 2016 7:13 am Apogee Perigee wrote: He may also (in a more Jungian sense) be acting out his own desire to be Shinji's Senex. This reveals more about Kaworu and his obvious self-sacrificing nature.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Apogee Perigee » Sun Nov 20, 2016 3:41 pm The problem I have with Adam being female isn't simply a wish for duality. My problem with Adam being female is that, for one, Rei is supposed to be the incarnation of Lilith. This makes sense. Lilith is undoubtedly female in the biblical texts and the fact that she is basically a clone of Yui helps that case. Lilith is basically God. In the original texts of the bible, the female is presented as the first one to eat the fruit of knowledge, so it makes sense that the God of humanity is female. However, when it comes to Adam (at least to me), it makes a lot more sense for him to be male. Almost all of the Angels in Christian mythology are male and Yahweh is undoubtedly presented as a male figure. Adam, being the God of Angels, opposing Lilith, is (Once more in my opinion) most likely male. It also makes sense as a parallel to Rei. Since it is most likely that Dr. Katsuragi is the progenitor of Kaworu (with him being a selfish father, just as Yui is a selfish mother) it would make sense that Adam would be male. Also I have no idea why Anno would name a female character male. Again, this is just my theory and if you have an answer that debunks my idea, I would be happy to listen to it.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Arcadia's legacy » Sun Nov 20, 2016 3:51 pm Apogee Perigee wrote: just as Yui is a selfish mother

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Apogee Perigee » Sun Nov 20, 2016 4:04 pm Another thing. On the note of LoL. Their ability is to pierce the ATF of any living thing. The idea of the LoLs is a protective device. The fact that it is impaled in Adam is not evidence of a form of cosmic sex. It is evidence of Lilith attempting to destroy Adam's ATF and therefore kill him. In EoE, the spear is used to destabilize the ATFs of Eva-01 and the S2 Engine. Then, through a process not yet explained, reforms them. To me, this is evidence of the LoL's ability to be both a destroyer and fixer. This is ultimately a very subjective show which is primarily left to the viewer. Until Anno releases something solid, we can never say for certainty. Instead, we choose the most likely choices. I will admit that Kaworu's "mother" line is odd, but keep in mind that Kaworu is likely as clueless as any other Angel, which are probably naive and unintelligent, being that they had no one to teach them but human minds (i.e. the Arael, Leliel and Armisael scenes.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Apogee Perigee » Sun Nov 20, 2016 4:05 pm Explain to me how she did that.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Arcadia's legacy » Sun Nov 20, 2016 4:14 pm Apogee Perigee wrote: Explain to me how she did that.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Apogee Perigee » Sun Nov 20, 2016 4:17 pm For one, I'm tired of your sarcasm. It doesn't make you look cool. Anyway, while, to an extent, the Actus reus was somewhat unselfish, the Mens rea was totally selfish and self-serving. All she wanted was to live forever. Even then, the Actus reus caused a huge amount of emotional damage to her son.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Arcadia's legacy » Sun Nov 20, 2016 4:36 pm Apogee Perigee wrote: All she wanted was to live forever. Even then, the Actus reus caused a huge amount of emotional damage to her son.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Apogee Perigee » Sun Nov 20, 2016 4:42 pm Well it worked did it not? Yui did not get harmed and her plan worked. If my theory about her being selfish is true, then it makes sense. Secondly, I'm using Actus reus to describe her actions and Mens rea to describe her intent.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Nov 20, 2016 4:47 pm Apogee : NGE is using Judeo-Christian symbolism for its own ends, so coming to conclusions based on the original usages while disregarding what the show itself is telling you is doing things backwards. @Kaworu being "clueless", keep in mind that Kaworu ACTUALLY HAS ADAM'S SOUL. He's able to direct Rei toward her own true nature, which is nonsensical if he doesn't even intuitively grasp his own. Kaworu's line about Adam and the Angels' desire to return to the Mother is also far too important in terms of establishing the full scope of certain NGE themes to so glibly dismiss. The argument for Angels being male and thus Kaworu must be has no substance, as traditional angels are supposed to be (pop culture distortions notwithstanding) sexless beings of pure spiritual energy. Suffice it to say that the Angels in NGE are very, very different from half their namesake (the other half being the Apostles, which is what they're called in Japanese). Not really sure where you're going with the Dr. Katsuragi thing; there is nothing in Eva requiring that parallels be so simplistically literal. Not to mention, you're disregarding the reversed-Jesus (anti-Christ?) origin here, wherein the Rei parallels break down, and only a female Adam balances out the "equation" as I previously described. You haven't addressed anywhere: the breasts on Kaworu's statue avatar, nor Kaworu being identified with three different mother figures during that scene in EoE (where he's also borrowing the body of yet another mother). This stuff was in the post I linked in case you didn't read it. On top of that, if you so strongly desire thematic cohesion in your Eva, how in the world does a male Adam fit in with the other creator beings and what they not so subtly represent?

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Arcadia's legacy » Sun Nov 20, 2016 4:48 pm Let us say you're correct and Immortality was indeed Yui's plan, now what? Is she just going to sit in Unit-01 for all eternity doing nothing? What exactly was meant to be achieved outside of living forever? This scenario doesn't sound very fufilling

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Apogee Perigee » Sun Nov 20, 2016 4:59 pm Reichu, your reply made a lot of sense. I do have some additional ideas, however. Kaworu directing Rei to her own nature could be explained by Seele informing him. Your other points made wa lot of sense.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Apogee Perigee » Mon Nov 21, 2016 3:26 am So this is an attempt to sort of present an opposing viewpoint to the omnipresent "ADAM IS FEMALE" sentiment. I'm new to this thing so I'm probably wrong, but I will be basing this as a response to Reichu's comment, which I think was well put together but wrong in certain aspects. Keep in mind that I am a beginner to theorizing this series and I'm probably wrong, LoL (see what I did there). Anyway for one, this statement "as traditional angels are supposed to be sexless beings of pure spiritual energy" is not true. Anno draws from the early European times of Christianity. This is clear from the crosses to the Lance of Longinus itself. In this European time of Christianity, Angels were ALWAYS depicted as male. Your second statement that Angels are different from their namesake is also not true. Even in appearance, some of the Angels embody religious appearances. Sachiel with its multiple heads signifying the Cherub. The blinding light of Arael is a clear reference to the Seraphim. The many-eyed, circular-shaped appearance of Matariel is a reference to Thrones. The same is true with Armisael. Now I'm not just bitterly correcting you here. The Angels and their appearances are clearly drawn from European-Christian origins. In said mythology, Angels are male. Signifying the gender of the Angels? Not necessarily. To me, it is more likely that this shows a male origin, providing a different sort of parallel. The Angels, due to the loss of their father, are vicious and wicked, providing a sharp contrast to Shinji's emotional instability at the loss of his mother. It seems to me, that the show is building a comparison between the Angels, beings that lost a father and thus are vicious and wicked (my reasons for thinking this are the three mind-invading angels), and the Children, who all lack a mother figure and are thus emotionally unstable. This makes sense and even to an extent parallels one another. The numbers linked to their names is another comparison. Ultimately, if this hypothesis was true, it would make no sense for Adam to be female. Your next comment about the statue figure I cannot verify because I couldn't find a statue figure of Kaworu with breasts. Your next claim is that Kaworu is with mother figures is also untrue in my eyes. Kaworu is with 3 other figures:Gendo, Yui and Rei. These are all parental figures, not motherly figures. You might say "Gendo never acted as a parent to Shinji". The thing is, neither did Yui. Lastly, I have one more question, what other creator figures are you referring to? Lilith is a singular entity, and a father fits perfectly with a mother. I am not trying to start an argument so please don't take this personally. I see you as a very intelligent person. I just wish to discuss something with someone with opposing viewpoints. Hope this flares your ideas up and we can have a conversation!

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Apogee Perigee » Mon Nov 21, 2016 3:52 am I just realized you were talking about the statue Kaworu was sitting on. The thing about that is that it is a reference to the Winged Victory Of Samothrace. This statue is a dedication to the goddess Nike, who served as a divine charioteer. Her job was giving glory to the victors. In the same way, Kaworu gives Shinji the victory of the battle willingly. Also, the figure is similar to the Harpies, which, based on the already-existing mythology, must be female. It would then make sense that it has breasts. This could also signify the fact that Kaworu's dummy plugs piloted the Harpies.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon Nov 21, 2016 7:53 am I think you are once again letting external sources and their meaning supercede the show's ability to create its own meaning as it sees fit. Calling "Adam as female" omnipresent makes it sound like it's the fan consensus or something, when it's mostly just me shouting occasionally into the void hoping to be heard. I'm not clear where you're getting Christian Medieval angels from. Much of the iconography comes from the Book of Enoch, a tome of Jewish apocrypha. The Angel's names source almost entirely from Judaic lore as well. The show is most certainly using the "androgynous spirit-being" concept as well, given the way the Angels are depicted with otherworldly forms that defy sexual categorization, and have a physicality along the lines of energy made solid. If Anno had wanted Adam to represent a father, he had a perfect opportunity to declare his intentions, but instead voided expectations by describing Adam as a mother instead. The language Kaworu used even evokes the way "God the Father" is written in Japanese, making the switch feel all the more intentional. This is a late-stage revelation that is never contradicted, so logically it was included to build the mythos, rather than to force fans into figuring out how to creatively disregard it -- surely? The scene in EoE that I was referring to is the one where the giant Kaworu appears. In short order, the scene creates parallels between Kaworu and with Kyoko, Yui, and Misato. You shouldn't have to take my word for it, so when I'm not feeling so under the weather I'll make a post detailing these parallels more.

[]: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Apogee Perigee » Mon Nov 21, 2016 1:00 pm Saying that the Angels have androgynous appearances is true with Adam as well. If anything, Giant of Light Adam is more similar to a male then a female but that probably doesn't mean anything. As for Kaworu calling Adam his mother.....well I can't really contradict that. One could argue that Adam and Lilith are genderless or perhaps even female. But Kaworu is in my eyes certainly a father-figure. This line proves it to me. "You betrayed me! You betrayed my mind! You betrayed like my father!"

Freud's father complex is a complex which mainly has to do with the (obvious) aspects of a father. These aspects normally include a close friendship and mentoring for boys.

Anyway for one, this statement "as traditional angels are supposed to be sexless beings of pure spiritual energy" is not true. Anno draws from the early European times of Christianity. This is clear from the crosses to the Lance of Longinus itself. In this European time of Christianity, Angels were ALWAYS depicted as male. Your second statement that Angels are different from their namesake is also not true. Even in appearance, some of the Angels embody religious appearances. Sachiel with its multiple heads signifying the Cherub. The blinding light of Arael is a clear reference to the Seraphim. The many-eyed, circular-shaped appearance of Matariel is a reference to Thrones. The same is true with Armisael. Now I'm not just bitterly correcting you here. The Angels and their appearances are clearly drawn from European-Christian origins. In said mythology, Angels are male. Signifying the gender of the Angels? Not necessarily. To me, it is more

Article: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis
ael. Now I'm not just bitterly correcting you here. The Angels and their appearances are clearly drawn from European-Christian origins. In said mythology, Angels are male. Signifying the gender of the Angels? Not necessarily. To me, it is more likely that this shows a male origin, providing a different sort of parallel. The Angels, due to the loss of their father, are vicious and wicked, providing a sharp contrast to Shinji's emotional instability at the loss of his mother. It seems to me, that the show is building a comparison between the Angels, beings that lost a father and thus are vicious and wicked (my reasons for thinking this are the three mind-invading angels), and the Children, who all lack a mother figure and are thus emotionally unstable. This makes sense and even to an extent parallels one another. The numbers linked to their names is another comparison. Ultimately, if this hypothesis was true, it would make no sense for Adam to be female. []: Re: Kaworu as father and Rei as mother hypothesis             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon Nov 21, 2016 7:53 am I think you are once again letting external sources and their meaning supercede the show's ability to create its own meaning as it sees fit. Calling "Adam as female" omnipresent makes it sound like it's the fan consensus or something, when it's mostly just me shouting occasionally into the void hoping to be heard. I'm not clear where you're getting Christian Medieval angels from. Much of the iconography comes from the Book of Enoch, a tome of Jewish apocrypha. The Angel's names source almost entirely from Judaic lore as well. The show is most certainly using the "androgynous spirit-being" concept as well, given the way the Angels are depicted with otherworldly forms that defy sexual categorization, and have a physicality along the lines of energy made solid. If Anno had wanted Adam to represent a father, he had a perfect opportunity to declare his intentions, but instead voided expectations by describing Adam as a mother instead. The language Kaworu used even evokes the way "God the Father" is written in Japanese, making the switch feel all the more intentional. This is a late-stage revelation that is never contradicted, so logically it was included to build the mythos, rather than to force fans into figuring out how to creatively disregard it -- surely? The scene in EoE that I was referring to is the one where the giant Kaworu appears. In short order, the scene creates parallels between Kaworu and with Kyoko, Yui, and Misato. You shouldn't have to take my word for it, so when I'm not feeling so under the weather I'll make a post detailing these parallels more.
